content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Marcia L. Scully 
General Counsel 
Office of Ethics 
Box 54153 
Los Angeles, CA 90054-0153 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-011 
Dear Ms. Scully: 
March 3. 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Metropolitan Water District 
Board Members Glen Dake and Laura Friedman regarding conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby 
( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ). and any advice we provide therefore assumes that the facts you provided 
to us are complete and accurate. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Board members Dake and Friedman participate in governmental decisions about the 
Metropolitan Water District's (Metropolitan) budget and rates, including financial 
incentives for turf removal and drought-tolerant landscaping, even though both Directors 
have financial interests in landscaping businesses that perform these services? 
2. May Director Dake avoid what otherwise might be a conflict of interest by renouncing any 
financial benefits from Metropolitan during his term in office? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Both Directors Dake and Friedman have a disqualifying conflict of interest that prohibits 
them from participating in the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget and rates, 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9!014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 IO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-011 
Page No. 2 
because such decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their 
economic interest in landscaping firms. 
2. If Director Dake completely and unequivocally renounces any financial benefits from 
Metropolitan, he may participate in the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget and 
rates, because it will not have a reasonable material financial effect on his economic interest 
in his landscaping firm. 
FACTS 
You are the General Counsel for Metropolitan and you write on behalf of board members 
Dake and Friedman who both serve on Metropolitan' s Board of Directors, a 38-member elected 
body representing each of the district's 26 member agencies. Director Dake is also a co-owner and 
officer of GDML Holdings, Inc. (dba DakeLuna), a landscape architecture business whose services 
include converting grass turf to drought-tolerant landscaping. Director Friedman's spouse, 
Guillaume Lemoine, owns Picture This Land ("PTL"), a landscape design and installation firm 
whose services also include turf removal and drought tolerant-landscaping. All of PTL's work 
comes from within Metropolitan's jurisdiction, while 95% of DakeLuna's work originates within 
the District. 
Metropolitan offers financial incentives to ratepayers who carry out certain water­
conserving measures, such as replacing turf with drought-tolerant landscaping or installing water­
saving sprinklers. Property owners can apply directly for these incentives themselves; contractors, 
including landscaping firms, may also apply on behalf of their clients. Metropolitan is currently in 
the process of its biennial budget approval and rate-setting process, with the Board expected to vote 
on a final budget and rates in April 2016. Past budgets have included water conservation incentives 
as part of a single "demand management" line item, but turf removal incentives are likely to be a 
topic of discussion and voting at one or more Board meetings throughout the current budget and 
rate-setting process. 
In the past, DakeLuna has accepted turf removal work that was partially funded with 
incentives from Metropolitan. In 2014, DakeLuna performed work as a subcontractor on a City of 
Los Angeles stormwater project that was funded with incentives from Metropolitan. But since 
Director Dake joined Metropolitan's Board, DakeLuna has adopted a policy whereby it will not 
accept any work that involves funding, directly or indirectly, from Metropolitan during Director 
Dake's tenure. 
One of PTL's clients also received an incentive from Metropolitan's turf removal program 
in connection with a landscape redesign that PTL carried out in 2015. PTL, however, does not 
actively seek work or funding from Metropolitan, nor has the business received any funds directly 
from Metropolitan in the past. Mr. Lemoine estimates that less than I% of the business's work 
currently comes from government incentives and funding. 
The issue is now whether Director Dake and Director Friedman, due to their interests in 
landscaping businesses, may participate in discussions about Metropolitan's water conservation 
incentives and funding, or whether they may participate in Metropolitan's budget and rate-setting 
process altogether. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-16-011 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the official's interests. (Section 87103.) 
Based on the facts you provided, three types of financial interests under the Act are 
implicated: 
• A business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment 
of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, 
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87 l03(d), emphasis added.) 
• A source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or 
more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103( c ), emphasis 
added.) 
• Personal Finances - A public official has a financial interest in his or her 
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate familf This is 
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.t 
Director Dake thus has three potentially disqualifying interests: ( 1) DakeLuna as a business 
entity, in which he holds a direct investment and serves as an officer; (2) DakeLuna as a source of 
income; and (3) DakeLuna's clients of at least $500 as a source of income. (See, e.g. Co11dotti 
Advice Letter, No. A-15-198.). 
Director Friedman also has three potentially disqualifying interests: ( 1) an indirect 
investment in her spouse's business, PTL;> (2) PTL as a source of income, to the extent that her 
community property interest is $500 or more. (See, e.g. Veil Advice Letter, No. A-15-126.); and (3) 
PTL's clients of at least $500 as a source of income. 
Therefore, to determine whether Director Dake or Director Friendman has a conflict of 
interest under the Act, we must determine whether the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's 
budget and rates would have a foreseeable, material financial effect on the Directors' interests. 
2 Where a governmental decision affects a business entity in which the official has a financial interest, the 
personal financial effect regulation does not apply and materiality is instead determined under Regulation I 8702.5(c). 
3 An individual's income under the Act also includes a pro rata share of the income of a business entity in 
which_ her spouse owns a JO percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).) 


Reasonable Foreseeability: 
File No. A-16-01 1 
Page No. 4 
To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of governmental decision on a 
public official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) Before 
deciding whether an effect on the board members' interests would be material, we must first 
determine whether any effects are foreseeable. A financial effect is reasonably foreseeable only if 
the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
(Regulation 1870l(b)). Put differently, if the financial result is unlikely absent extraordinary 
circumstances outside of the public official's control, it is 11ot reasonably foreseeable.4 
Dakel1111a and Picture This land. 
In this case, there is a realistic possibility that the Board's decisions about turf removal 
incentives could have a financial effect on DakeLuna or PTL, because both businesses are in the 
business of replacing turf with drought-tolerant landscaping. 
However, in the context of business or income interests we have previously determined that 
a public official may avoid disqualification if he or she "completely and unequivocally renounces 
any intent to avail him/herself of the material financial benefit giving rise to a conflict of interest." 
(See, e.g. Walter Advice Letter I-09-275, Thompson Advice Letter, No. A-02-024, and Newsom 
Advice Letter, No. A-01-183.) 
You have indicated that DakeLuna has established a policy under which the business does 
not accept any work funded directly or indirectly by Metropolitan. Thus, if DakeLuna completely 
and unequivocally renounces any intent to receive business funded by money from Metropolitan, 
there is no foreseeable material financial effect on Director Dake' s business, and Director Dake 
may participate in the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget and rates. 
Sources of Income 
You stated that none of DakeLuna and PTL's currem cliellfs (or cliems within the past 12 
mollfhs) are seeking incentives or funding from Metropolitan. Therefore, it is not foreseeable that 
the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget would have a material effect on Director Dake 
or Director Friedman's sources of income. We do not analyze these further. 
Materiality: 
Because it is foreseeable that the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget and rates 
could affect the PTL, we must then determine whether that effect would be material. An effect on a 
business interest is material if a "prudent person with sufficient information would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price 
of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity." 
4 There is a different test where an interest is explicitly involved in a decision, which does not apply to the 
Directors' business interests, since neither the Directors or the businesses are a named party in or the subject of the 
Board's decision. (See Regulation 1870 I (a).) 


File No. A-16-011 
Page No. 5 
(Regulation 1 8702.1 (b ))Two relevant examples of decisions that would affect the value of a public 
official's privately-held business are decisions that: 
"( 1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which 
the business entity is engaged;" 
"(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity 
supplies." 
Based on the facts you provided, Metropolitan's budget and rate-setting process will 
establish conditions for turf removal and instal1ation of drought-tolerant landscaping. Likewise, you 
observe that demand for these services is expected to grow, and that this increase in demand "may 
involve services from tile landscaping industry such as those provided by PTL." Thus, under the 
prudent person standard, we find it reasonably foreseeable that the Board's decisions about the 
demand-management elements of Metropolitan's budget could contribute to a change in the value 
of PTL. 
Segmentation of budget decisions: 
The Commission has advised that "[u]nder certain circumstances, a public official 
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions provided that the official's 
participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conf1ict of interest." (In re Owen 
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) We have also advised that it is possible to "segment" budget decisions in 
this way. To do so, a public agency must decide matters in which an official has a disqualifying 
interest separately from and before allowing the conflicted official to vote on the other matters. 
(Ginsburg Advice Letter, No. 1-13-081.) Such segmentation is permissible even if the final budget 
includes the items from which the official was disqualified, provided that the decision does not 
reopen or in any way alter the decisions from which he or she was disqualified. (Cook Advice 
Letter, No. A-83-163.) 
But Regulation 18706 provides that, for such "segmentation" to be permitted, "the decision 
in which the official has a financial interest [must be capable of being] broken down into separate 
decisions that are not inextricably ill!errelated to the decision in which the official has a 
disqualifying financial interest." (1 8706(a)(l).) As such, we have also previously advised that some 
decisions may be too interrelated to be considered separately. If the resolution of one decision will 
effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the other decision, the decisions may not 
be segmented. (See generally Yang Advice Letter, No. 1-06-198; Stone Advice Letter, No. A-06-
007; Barker Advice Letter, A-05; Hull Advice Letter, No. A-04-052.) 
While we provide the aforementioned guidelines on segmentation, determinations about 
segmenting budget decisions are ultimately Metropolitan's purview. You have indicated that in the 
case of Metropolitan' s budget and rate-setting process, segmentation would not be possible.• Thus, 
Directors Dake and Friedman may not avoid a conflict of interest by segmenting the budget 
decisions concerning demand management incentives from Metropolitan's larger budget and rate­
setting process. 


File No. A-16-01 1 
Page No. 6 
The "legally required" and "public generally" exceptions do not apply 
The facts do not suggest that either Director Dake or Director Friedman's participation is 
legally required (see Regulation 18705) or that the public generally exception might apply 
(Regulation 18703). Therefore, we do not address these exceptions. 
:jg] 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
By: 
Toren A. Lewis 
Counsel, Legal Division

them from participating in the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget and rates,because such decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their 
economic interest in landscaping firms. 
2. If Director Dake completely and unequivocally renounces any financial benefits from

in his landscaping firm.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Board members Dake and Friedman participate in governmental decisions about the 
Metropolitan Water District's (Metropolitan) budget and rates, including financial 
incentives for turf removal and drought-tolerant landscaping, even though both Directors 
have financial interests in landscaping businesses that perform these services? 
2. May Director Dake avoid what otherwise might be a conflict of interest by renouncing any 
financial benefits from Metropolitan during his term in office?

CONCLUSION: 1. Both Directors Dake and Friedman have a disqualifying conflict of interest that prohibits 
them from participating in the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget and rates,because such decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their 
economic interest in landscaping firms. 
2. If Director Dake completely and unequivocally renounces any financial benefits from 
Metropolitan, he may participate in the Board's decisions about Metropolitan's budget and 
rates, because it will not have a reasonable material financial effect on his economic interest 
in his landscaping firm.
